1: Wayəhî kəkallôt šəlōmōh libənôt — “when Solomon had finished building.” The verb kālāh (finish, complete) brackets the entire building program: 7:51 used the same word for the individual structures; here it applies to the whole complex (Temple + palace + everything he desired, ḥēpeṣ). The completion of the program creates the structural conditions for YHWH’s second appearance — divine response to completed human faithfulness.
2: Wayyērāʾ yhwh ʾel-šəlōmōh šēnît — “the LORD appeared to Solomon a second time.” The first appearance was at Gibeon before the Temple project (3:5-15). The symmetry is deliberate: YHWH appeared to commission Solomon at the beginning of his reign, and appears again to evaluate and condition its continuation at the Temple’s completion. The narrator’s kaʾăšer nirʾāh ʾêlāyw bəgiḇʿôn — “as he had appeared to him at Gibeon” — signals the reader to compare the two appearances as a pair.
3: Šāmaʿtî ʾet-tepillatǝkā wǝʾet-taḥinatǝkā ʾăšer hitḥannantā lǝpānāy — “I have heard your prayer and your plea that you made before me.” YHWH’s first word is confirmation: the dedication prayer was received. Qiddaštî ʾet-habbayit hazzeh ʾăšer bānîtā lāśûm-šəmî šām ʿaḏ-ʿôlām — “I have consecrated this house to put my name there forever.” Qādaš (consecrate, make holy) in the Piel — YHWH himself performs the consecration, not Solomon’s ritual. The Temple is holy because YHWH has declared it so, not because of the building’s quality.
4: The conditional covenant resumes: wəʾattāh ʾim-tēlek lǝpānay kāʾăšer hālek dāwid ʾāḇîkā — “as for you, if you walk before me as your father David walked.” The verb hālak lǝpānāy (walk before me) is the key covenant-walking idiom: it describes the totality of covenant life lived under YHWH’s gaze. Bǝtom-lēḇāḇ ûḇəyōšer — “with integrity of heart and uprightness.” Tōm (wholeness, completeness) and yōšer (uprightness, straightness) define Davidic covenant faithfulness. Both terms are internal — the condition is not merely external observance.
5: Wăhăqîmōtî ʾet-kissēʾ mamlaktǝkā ʿal-yiśrāʾēl lǝʿōlām — “I will establish your royal throne over Israel forever.” The promise of perpetual dynasty (lǝʿōlām) was given unconditionally to David in 2 Samuel 7:13-16. Here it is given conditionally to Solomon — a key distinction in Deuteronomistic theology. The unconditional promise guarantees the dynasty will not finally fail; the conditional form governs whether individual kings participate in its blessing. The tension between unconditional (Davidic) and conditional (Mosaic) covenant strands runs through all of Kings.
6: ʾIm-šôḇ tāšûḇûn ʾattem ûḇənêkem mēʾaḥărāy — “but if you utterly turn away, you and your sons, from following me.” The emphatic double infinitive šôḇ tāšûḇûn (turn, you will turn) intensifies the warning. The apostasy envisioned is deliberate and complete, not accidental or partial. The condition is relational abandonment — mēʾaḥărāy (from following me) — not merely ritual failure. The covenant is broken by turning away from YHWH himself, not merely by neglecting individual commandments.
7: Wəhikrattî ʾet-yiśrāʾēl mēʿal pənê hāʾădāmāh — “I will cut off Israel from the face of the land.” The verb kārat (cut) is the covenant word: to be cut off from the covenant is to be cut off from the land. Wəhabbayit ʾăšer qiddaštî lišmî ʾašallaḥ mēʿal pānāy — “the house I have consecrated for my name I will cast from my sight.” Šālaḥ mēʿal pānāy (cast from before my face) — the Temple’s consecration is reversible. What YHWH dedicated he can also un-dedicate. This is the theological preparation for the destruction narrated in 2 Kings 25.
8: Wǝhabbayit hazzeh yihyeh ʿelyôn — the house will become a ruin (MT reads “high/exalted” but the context requires desolation; some mss and LXX support “heap of ruins”). Kol-ʿōḇēr ʿālāyw yiššōm wəyišrōq — “everyone who passes by it will be appalled and will hiss.” The desolation of Zion becomes a theological spectacle for the nations — the very nations who were invited to pray toward the house will pass by its ruins in horror. Jeremiah uses this exact language of hissing-and-amazement (Jer 18:16; 19:8).
9: Wəʾāmərû ʿal-meh ʿāśāh yhwh kākāh lāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt ûlabbayit hazzeh — “they will say: why did the LORD do this to this land and this house?” The question the nations will ask and the answer they will receive constitute a brief catechism: sin → abandonment → judgment. Wəhiḥězîqû bēʾlōhîm ʾăḥērîm — “because they held on to other gods.” The root of the catastrophe is not political or military but covenantal. The Temple’s destruction will serve as testimony to the nations of the same monotheistic truth the Temple’s dedication prayed for (8:60).
10: Solomon gave Hiram of Tyre twenty cities in Galilee (Gālîl) in payment for timber and gold. The geographic designation ʾereṣ haggālîl appears here for the first time in Scripture. Isaiah 9:1 designates this same region (“Galilee of the nations”) as the place where the people sitting in darkness will see a great light — the very territory alienated by Solomon becomes the setting for Jesus’ Galilean ministry.
11: Hiram’s inspection of the cities and his verdict: mah-hehārim hāʾēlleh ʾăšer nātattāh lî ʾāḥî — “what are these cities you have given me, my brother?” The irony of ʾāḥî (my brother) — treaty-partnership language — combined with the dismissive question creates a tense diplomatic moment. The relationship with Tyre that was so productive for the Temple project ends with a rebuke of Solomon’s payment. The narrator records it without exculpating Solomon.
12: Hiram named the cities ʾereṣ Kāḇûl — the land of Cabul, meaning possibly “good-for-nothing” or “like nothing” (interpreted as wordplay on kā-ḇal, meaning “as nothing”). The naming is an act of contempt, recorded in the text as permanent memory of the transaction. The Temple was built with Hiram’s premium materials; Solomon’s payment was substandard. The narrator does not editorialize, but the contrast is stark.
13: Wayyišlaḥ ləhamelek meʾāh wəʿeśrîm kikkār zāhāb — Hiram sent 120 talents of gold. Despite his displeasure, the alliance continues. The transaction is completed, the gold is paid. The larger economic framework of Solomon’s reign — massive imports funded by tribute, trade, and alienation of land — is starting to accumulate. By ch11, the weight of these obligations will be visible in the structural cracks of his kingdom.
14: The 120 talents of gold are the only explicit payment figure for the Hiram alliance. Whether this represents full settlement or partial payment is not specified. The brevity of the record invites the reader to hold the exchange in mind — an international partnership for the Temple required significant and apparently imperfect earthly economics.
15: Wəzeh dəḇar-hammās ʾăšer heʿĕlāh hammelek šəlōmōh — “this is the account of the forced labor (hammās) that King Solomon levied.” Mas (forced labor, levy, work-gang) is the same word used for Israel’s labor under Pharaoh in Exodus 1:11 — śārê missîm (taskmasters of the forced labor). The verbal echo is not accidental. Solomon’s building program, for all its theological grandeur, uses the infrastructure of oppression. The complaint that will break apart the kingdom in ch12 is rooted here.
16: The list of building projects: Jerusalem’s Millo (hammillōʾ, the fill/terrace structure), the wall, Hazor, Megiddo, Gezer. Pharaoh had captured and burned Gezer, killing its Canaanite inhabitants, and gave it as a dowry to Solomon with his daughter. The international alliance that enables the Temple project is simultaneously a political-dynastic arrangement that brings Egyptian royal daughters into the Davidic household — an entanglement that 11:1-8 will identify as Solomon’s undoing.
17: Gezer, Lower Beth-horon, Baalath — the construction program continues down the Shephelah and into the coastal plain. The scale of the building program required the forced labor levy of v15. The geographic spread demonstrates Solomon’s administrative reach across the whole land, but also the human cost of maintaining it.
18: Tadmor in the wilderness (possibly Palmyra, the great desert caravan city) and all the storage cities of Solomon. The extension of Solomon’s building to Tadmor, far northeast of Israel, represents the empire’s maximal geographic extent — a detail preserved here as evidence of unprecedented scale.
19: The ʿārê hammiskənôt (storage cities), chariot cities, and cavalry cities, and “all of Solomon’s desires that he desired to build.” The phrase kol-ḥēšeq šəlōmōh ʾăšer ḥāšaq — all his desire that he desired — is the language of appetite. The same word (ḥēšeq) appears in 9:1 for the things he desired to build. The echo suggests that Solomon’s construction program is driven by personal appetite as much as covenant obligation.
20: The forced labor of vv20-22 carefully distinguishes its source: kol-hāʿām hannôtār min-hāʾĕmōrî hahittî happərizzî haḥiwwî wəhayyəḇûsî ʾăšer lōʾ-mibənê yiśrāʾēl hēmmāh — all the remaining Canaanite peoples who were not Israelites. The Israelites were exempt from the permanent-slave labor (v22). The structural distinction matters for the narrative: the temporary labor levy in 5:13-14 applied to Israel; the permanent slave-labor applies to the remnant Canaanites, fulfilling the conquest’s unfinished business (Joshua 17:13).
21: Bənêhem ʾăšer nôtərû ʾaḥărêhem bāʾāreṣ — “their descendants who remained in the land.” The hereditary nature of the slave-labor force: conquest-survivors and their children. Wayaʿălēm šəlōmōh ləmas ʿōḇēd — Solomon imposed on them a slave-labor force that remains to this day. The parenthetical “to this day” is the narrator’s signal that this arrangement was still operating when the history was written.
22: But from the Israelites (ûmibbənê yiśrāʾēl lōʾ-nātan šəlōmōh ʿāḇed) Solomon made no slaves. They were the military corps: men of war, commanders, officers, cavalry-chiefs. The Israelite/Canaanite distinction in the forced-labor system is preserved as a matter of record. But the record of 5:13 (a 30,000-man labor levy from Israel) complicates the clean picture, suggesting that the distinction was not always honored in practice.
23: The administrative structure: 550 overseers (śārê hannǝṣiḇîm) over the workforce, supervising the labor. The Hebrew gives both 550 (here) and 3,300 in 5:16 — a discrepancy that Chronicles reconciles by giving a combined figure. The administrative personnel required to manage Solomon’s building program was itself substantial, a sub-bureaucracy of supervisors over workers.
24: Pharaoh’s daughter moved from the City of David to her own house that Solomon had built for her, after which Solomon built the Millo. The sequence suggests the Millo was completed as part of the palace complex for his Egyptian wife. The narrator notes this without comment, but the placement — immediately after the labor-levy discussion — frames the Egyptian marriage as part of the economic-political entanglement that required the forced labor program.
25: Three times a year Solomon offered burnt offerings and peace offerings on the altar he built to YHWH — presumably at the three pilgrimage festivals (Passover, Weeks, Tabernacles; cf. Deut 16:16). Wəhiqṭîr ʾittô ʾăšer ʿal-pənê yhwh — “and burning incense before YHWH.” Solomon’s personal cultic performance is noted as regular and correct — he fulfilled the prescribed ritual calendar. The narrator’s notice is bittersweet: the king who will introduce apostate shrines in ch11 is, at this point, still keeping the covenant liturgical calendar.
26: Solomon built a fleet at Ezion-geber (ʿeṣyôn geber) on the shore of the Reed Sea (yam sûp) in the land of Edom. Ezion-geber (modern Aqaba) is the northern tip of the Gulf of Aqaba — the maritime route to Arabia, East Africa, and India. David had conquered Edom (2 Sam 8:14), giving Solomon access to this port. The Red Sea fleet represents the empire’s commercial ambition made geographic: the domain of David’s military conquest becomes the base of Solomon’s commercial enterprise.
27: Hiram sent his servants alongside Solomon’s servants: ʾanšê yōdəʿê hayyām — men who knew the sea. The Israelites were not historically a maritime people (unlike Phoenicia); the partnership with Hiram provided the expertise. The shared fleet is a joint venture — Solomon’s gold, Hiram’s seamanship. The economic partnership that built the Temple is extended to global commerce.
28: The fleet went to Ophir (ʾôpîr) and brought back 420 talents of gold. Ophir’s location is uncertain (Somalia, south Arabia, India, and Zimbabwe have all been proposed), but “gold of Ophir” became a byword for the finest gold in the ancient world (Ps 45:9; Job 22:24; Isa 13:12). The 420 (or 450, per 2 Chr 8:18) talents represent an enormous haul. The empire’s wealth is as much international-commercial as it is tribute-based.
